title: House and Senate split on votes to rebuke Trump on Iran war
description: House lawmakers voted to cease the conflict, while the Senate stalled a similar effort just hours later.
fullText:
Congress delivered President Donald Trump a mixed message Thursday on his handling of the war in Iran, just as hostilities flare in the Middle East.

The two chambers split on whether to halt the war in a pair of hotly contested ballots. House Democrats — aided by a handful of Republicans — succeeded for a second time in rebuking Trump over the military campaign. But the Senate GOP rejected a similar effort just hours later, as Democrats couldn't muster the needed support to advance another anti-war measure.

The dueling votes highlight the challenges lawmakers face in reining in Trump and stopping the nearly five-month conflict.

The House vote is another sign that lawmakers are increasingly unhappy with the conflict, as peace talks founder and the two sides sharply escalated hostilities in recent weeks — resulting in the deaths of four U.S. military personnel in the Middle East over the weekend. It also comes as Republicans attempt to hold onto their narrow majority in the House in the November elections.

But the Senate's lack of action underscores the myriad hurdles facing Democrats, and some Republicans, as they weigh an endgame for the conflict. Both the House and Senate cleared legislation to end the war in June. Trump is certain to ignore those votes and is likely to do so again.

The resolutions are legally untested and largely symbolic. The House measure would not go to Trump to sign into law, and the president would almost certainly veto any binding legislation that restricts his authority.

Four Republicans broke ranks to help approve a Democratic-led war powers resolution in a 214-208 vote. It was the second time the House has narrowly rejected the renewed war. Four Republicans who previously voted to end the war did so Thursday: Reps. Tom Barrett of Michigan, Warren Davidson of Ohio, Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania and Thomas Massie of Kentucky.

The Senate followed by rejecting an effort to advance its own anti-war resolution, offered by Sen. Chris Van Hollen (D-Md.), in a 47-49 vote. Just one Republican, Sen. Susan Collins on Maine, backed the effort, while Democratic Sen. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania opposed it. Two Republicans who previously supported legislation to end the war, Sens. Rand Paul of Kentucky and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, did not vote.

House Democrats touted the victory, which again demonstrated the minority party can pick off enough Republicans to hand Trump an embarrassing loss. Democrats have relied on the war powers measures — one of the few tools at their disposal — to force Republicans on the record over the unpopular conflict, as the GOP risks being blamed for high gas prices just months ahead of midterm elections.

"What this reflects is that we will continue to put pressure on this president to stand up and to stop this war," said Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.), the sponsor of the resolution. "Working in a bipartisan way, we want to make sure that all the Republicans stand up for the Constitution, for their conscience, and for their constituents."

The war, launched in February by the U.S. and Israel, is now in its fifth month and shows no signs of ending soon. A shaky ceasefire first declared in April collapsed earlier this month, as the U.S. and Iran traded attacks and Trump reimposed a naval blockade of Iran's ports.

The White House had argued that the ceasefire stopped a 60-day deadline to end the conflict without congressional buy-in under the War Powers Resolution. The president then notified lawmakers last week that the nation was once again at war with Tehran, in a bid to reset the 60-day clock. Critics contend that the administration's justification is a misreading of the war powers law, noting that the U.S. has engaged in hostilities — namely a naval blockade — even during the ceasefire.

Despite the handful of GOP defections, most Republicans stuck with Trump.

"There were a small number of Republicans that joined us to pass this bill, but it is important to note that the vast majority of Republicans did not follow their consciences and did not listen to their constituents, and voted instead just to keep on spending billions more dollars on this war," said Rep. Greg Casar (D-Texas), who chairs the Congressional Progressive Caucus.

House Foreign Affairs Chair Brian Mast (R-Fla.) said the vote shows "that you have a number of members that have zero recognition" for the sacrifice of U.S. troops and the attacks by Iran in the region.

Leo Shane contributed to this report.

‘I’m Aiming For A Knockout’ – Ryujin Nasukawa Promises A Debut Fans Won’t Forget At The Inner Circle 23
description: Confidence meets aggression in this kickboxing war that promises fireworks in Lumpinee Stadium on July 24.
fullText:
ISKA World Champion Ryujin “Shindo” Nasukawa has made it clear he wants to lead kickboxing’s next era, and that mission gets underway at The Inner Circle 23 in Bangkok’s Lumpinee Stadium this Friday, July 24.

The 20-year-old Japanese phenom opens his account in the world’s largest martial arts organization against Thai slugger Thongpoon PK Saenchai in a strawweight kickboxing showdown. The card will be available exclusively to subscribers at live.onefc.com.

Stepping onto the global stage for the first time carries a weight that no amount of preparation can fully simulate.

Every athlete who has walked this same path, regardless of pedigree or hype, has had to face that same unknown the moment fight week finally arrives. That reality is no different for Ryujin, who is the younger brother of now-retired undefeated Japanese kickboxing great Tenshin Nasukawa.

Confidence has defined nearly everything he has said leading up to this moment, but even the most self-assured fighters admit there is a human side to a debut this significant.

“It still hasn’t really hit me yet. I honestly won’t know whether I’ll be nervous or not until fight day actually comes.”

Those nerves, whenever they arrive, will not catch Ryujin unprepared.

Thongpoon’s style is no secret to anyone who has followed his run inside ONE Championship, and the young gun has put in the hours breaking down exactly what he brings into the ring.

That preparation has left him with a clear, specific read on his opponent’s approach.

“He comes forward relentlessly and doesn’t seem to care about taking punches to get in close.”

That read on Thongpoon’s aggressive style has only sharpened the Chiba native’s own intentions heading into the biggest chapter of his career yet.

True to the explosive reputation that built his 18-3 record in the first place, Ryujin has no interest in simply getting his hand raised. He wants to announce himself with a debut that will long live in the memory of fight fans everywhere.

“I’m aiming for a knockout. I want people to think, ‘Something crazy just showed up.’

“I want a finish that goes beyond what everyone’s expecting. I want to bring a fresh wind to ONE and to the combat sports world as a whole.”

A statement finish in his promotional debut would be only the beginning of what Ryujin Nasukawa hopes to accomplish in ONE Championship.

The 20-year-old has spent his career chasing gold across multiple promotions, having claimed an ISKA World Title and becoming a two-division RISE Champion along the way. And he has no intention of slowing down now that he has arrived on the grandest stage for kickboxing.

Rather than treat strawweight as the ceiling, he sees it as just the starting point of something much bigger.

“I really want to win, leave a strong impact, and show that I have what it takes to chase World Titles in both the strawweight and atomweight divisions.”

While Friday marks his first promotional appearance as a competitor, it is far from his first taste of life inside the world’s largest martial arts organization.

Ryujin sat in the corner for fellow countrymen Sora Tanazawa and Kazuteru Yamazaki at ONE Friday Fights 158 and The Inner Circle 18 this past June, and he witnessed how electric the atmosphere can be at Lumpinee Stadium.

Now, he gets to experience that same energy firsthand, with the hope of one day competing at events like ONE SAMURAI as well.

“Everyone was so fired up. It didn’t matter which fighter won or lost, the crowd was electric [during the show last month]. It really made me realize what an intense stage this is.

“ONE SAMURAI is shining a spotlight on the Japanese stage, and for that, I’m really grateful on behalf of Japan’s combat sports scene. I think it’s a card that’s going to keep building momentum.”

title: 'Extraordinary' Iron Age war trumpet find in Britain may have Boudicca links
description: Bronze instrument or carnyx dug up in Norfolk in area inhabited by Celtic tribe led by warrior who fought Romans
fullText:
Bronze instrument or carnyx dug up in Norfolk in area inhabited by Celtic tribe led by warrior who fought Romans

An “extraordinary” iron age war trumpet that may have links to the Celtic tribe led by Boudicca in the period they were battling the invading Roman army has been discovered by archaeologists in Norfolk.

The bronze trumpet or carnyx is only the third ever found in Britain, and the most complete example discovered anywhere in the world. Fashioned in the shape of a snarling wild animal, the object would have been mounted on a long mouthpiece high above the heads of warriors, allowing it to be sounded to intimidate the enemy in battle.

It was found last summer, during excavations ahead of a new housing development in west Norfolk, among a collection of iron age military objects, which also included a bronze boar’s head battle standard – a unique find in Britain – and five shield bosses.

Tantalisingly, the area where the hoard was discovered falls within the territory of the Iceni, the British tribe that in AD 60 staged a fierce but ultimately doomed rebellion led by Boudicca against the Roman army. Archaeologists believe the hoard was buried at some point in the first century AD.

Mark Hinman, chief executive of Pre-Construct Archaeology, whose experts made the discovery, said the hoard was “a once-in-a-lifetime, once-in-a-career find. I’ve been doing archaeology for over 40 years and I’ve never seen anything like this.”

While the artefacts are sensational in themselves, their possible connection to one of the few named figures of the British iron age make the find even more special, he said. “We are absolutely within the heartland of the Iceni – whether it was they who buried the hoard or not, we just don’t know.

“[But] almost certainly the items were buried in the first century AD, and that brings us closer to the Iceni and to Boudicca. They are of such quality that … any important people from the Iceni and other groups would have known these objects.”

The fragile artefacts are still undergoing conservation work before they can be examined in detail, but the potential link to the Iceni “is an obvious question and one that we will definitely be focusing on once we’re able to get closer to the objects,” said Hinman.

A number of Greek and Roman writers described warrior tribes in Britain, Gaul and elsewhere sounding carnyces to terrify their enemies. “Their trumpets are of a peculiar barbarian kind; they blow into them and produce a harsh sound which suits the tumult of war,” the Greek historian Diodorus Siculus wrote in the first century BC. The Gundestrup cauldron, found in Denmark and dating from the same period, depicts three players holding their horns aloft.

Part of a boar’s head carnyx, until now the only surviving example in Britain (after another was melted down by antiquarians), was discovered in 1816 in Moray, northern Scotland; it is now in the National Museum of Scotland (NMS).

Fraser Hunter, iron age and Roman curator at NMS and a leading authority on carnyces, described the new find as “extraordinary” and said it would “add enormously to our understanding of the iron age world. I’ve looked at carnyces from around Europe, and the full research and conservation of these incredibly fragile remains will reshape our view of sound and music in the iron age.”

The newly discovered carnyx shows signs of repair, said Hinman, indicating it was in use over a long period. While it was partially dismantled before being buried, with the shield bosses placed carefully on top, “the whole of the bell and the head are relatively complete – and it’s the only one that’s ever been found where they haven’t taken the ears off. It’s got these great big flappy ears which are wonderful, and they’re still in place.

“Objects like this remind us how little we know about so many different aspects of our past. These objects had names, people thought they were imbued by power. They may even have thought that they were alive at certain times in their existence – and all of the stories that go with them are lost.”

Looking at the carnyx, he said: “It’s hard to describe, but you kind of feel like it’s looking back at you. It’s quite a thing.”

Currently in the care of conservation experts at Norfolk Museums Service, the hoard’s legal status currently sits with a coroner who will decide on its future according to the Treasure Act 1996.

The discovery will be featured in the second episode of the new series of Digging for Britain, which will be broadcast on BBC Two on 14 January at 9pm.

title: Laura Loomer meets with Zelenskyy in Ukraine after major reversal on the war
description: Laura Loomer, an ardent ally of President Donald Trump with a reputation for incendiary social media commentary, met with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Thursday after a dramatic turnaroun...
fullText:
KYIV (AP) — Laura Loomer, an ardent ally of President Donald Trump with a reputation for incendiary social media commentary, met with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Thursday after a dramatic turnaround on the war that could have political reverberations back in Washington.

Loomer had spent years minimizing Russia's invasion of Ukraine and amplifying Russian propaganda to her nearly two million followers. This week she had a change of heart.

"Just experienced my first air raid in Ukraine. Sirens blaring. This is everyday for every Ukrainian," Loomer wrote on X. "I often said I don't care. Looking back, that wasn't very nice of me to say."

It was a stunning reversal for a woman who had spent years criticizing U.S. support for Ukraine's defense, declaring it "a country full of Nazi apologists" — a claim used by Russia to justify its yearslong invasion — and "led by a Jihadi apologist."

The skepticism of Ukraine has been shared by other high profile conservative influencers, including former Fox News host Tucker Carlson.

The meeting with Zelenskyy was confirmed by Dmytro Lytvyn, a spokesperson for the president, who said that no Ukrainian government institution had organized the visit. He added that Zelenskyy's office first learned that Loomer was in Ukraine through social media.

"It is really important that @LauraLoomer is in Ukraine and sees things with her own eyes as they are," Zelenskyy's office had written Tuesday.

"It takes courage to come to Ukraine and not be deterred by Russian propaganda or air raid sirens. Thank you, Laura," they added Thursday.

Loomer did not respond to a request for comment.

In addition to the meeting with Zelenskyy, Loomer also visited the Dormition Cathedral of Kyiv-Pechersk Lavra, one of the country's most significant religious landmarks, which came under Russian attack last month.

The attack on the monastic complex of golden domes in the heart of Kyiv, which is also known as the Monastery of the Caves, sparked a fire that caused significant interior and exterior damage, according to UNESCO. Zelenskyy had called it Moscow's "biggest crime yet against Christian culture."

Russia denied responsibility for the attack, claiming, without offering evidence, that the complex was hit by one of Ukraine's U.S.-made Patriot air defense missiles — saying that it might have veered off course due to its age.

In a video recorded from inside the church, Loomer accused Russian President Vladimir Putin of hypocrisy by portraying Russia as pro-Christian.

"I just don't think that you should be allowed to say that you're protecting Christianity if you're trying to drone strike Jesus Christ," she said.

Sergiy Kyslytsya and Tetyana Berezhna, two top Ukrainian officials, were among those who met with Loomer and joined her for the visit.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the trip.

title: Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8%
description: Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity.
fullText:
Global hunger declined for the third consecutive year, according to the Food and Agriculture Organization's (FAO) The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2026 report.

The new report estimates that 7.8 percent of the world's people were undernourished in 2025.

The FAO report notes that less than 2.5 percent of people living in the U.S., Canada, Eastern Asia, and Europe are undernourished. Therefore, its calculations chiefly encompass the extent of hunger in developing countries in Africa, Latin America, and the rest of Asia.

Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity in 12 out of 13 hunger hotspots identified by the World Food Programme and the FAO.

The drop to 7.8 percent is a huge improvement since 1970, when undernourishment afflicted nearly 35 percent of people living in developing countries.

The new estimate is down from 8.1 percent in 2024 and 8.5 percent in 2023. Overall, 645 million people were still facing hunger in 2025.

While that is good news, the new estimate remains above the pre-pandemic nadir of 7.2 percent in 2017. The FAO report projects that the level of hunger will decline by 20 percent during the rest of this decade, but that 510 million people will still be undernourished in 2030. This trend falls well short of the United Nations' sustainable development goal of zero hunger by 2030.

The post Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8% appeared first on Reason.com.

title: Red Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war Berlin
description:
Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”
Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.
 Continue reading...
fullText:
Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades

‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”

Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.

You play as an (initially human) inhabitant of cold war Berlin who has tried to no avail to endure the conflict outside the window: through partying, journalling, activism … Still, you feel empty. When offered a new life of power and purpose, you don’t need much convincing to offer up your exposed neck.

“I think a lot of people who create vampire games feel the anguish and the melancholy of the genre,” says Van der Schilden. “Like, how to deal with your feeling of justice in an unjust world.”

In this tactical narrative RPG, you plunge into a fictional 80s Berlin by dispatching vampire agents to its fractured streets. They’re your eyes and ears, looking for clues, building reputation with city factions, and of course quenching their bloodthirst. As in last year’s superhero caper Dispatch (from AdHoc Studio), you’ll come across opportunities around the map, each with its own requirements, making use of a skillcheck system tied to six character skills. It’s up to you to pick the best fangs for the job, all while maintaining their energy, money and blood supply.

The vampires under your command are the beating (undead) heart of the experience. You progressively unveil their backstories by undertaking personal missions, during which you learn about their individual pasts and struggles. It’s in these moments that you begin to enact change and share some of your newfound purpose with others as guidance, deciding how their stories are ultimately resolved.

Wispfire emphasises the importance of the cast by infusing hand-drawn portraits with voice acting and motion-capture (mocap) animation. Notably, for the latter the team is using Live2D, a relatively inexpensive software tool commonly used by VTubers to animate their virtual avatars during a livestream.

A good iPhone was enough to start experimenting, but the team had to develop tools to adapt the software to suit their needs, and the tech isn’t without limitations. “With a mocap character for 3D, you usually can move your entire head,” says art director and animator Aïda de Ridder. “There’s something stuck to your face and it moves with you, right? We have a static phone, so we have to make their performance more centred. There is a limit to the range of movements.”

In order to make models feel “animated”, the team found that a performer needs to exaggerate their facial features to convey the desired result. But Wispfire is well acquainted with this, thanks to a shared background in interactive theatre where, as Van der Schilden says, “It’s harder to see for people in the back if you do a muted performance.”

Vampires shine the most when they have strong personalities and distinct looks. In Red Kiss, many elements intersect with one another to give characters power and purpose. And this is all being funnelled through a queer and dark lens – like the best vampire stories are.

“We like queer media, “says Van der Schilden. “But we, as a team, always felt that a lot of the media was very pink rainbows, happy-go-lucky. We wanted to make a really dark, queer, diverse game. And I think Red Kiss is that.”

Red Kiss is currently in development for PC, with a release date to be announced.

title: IDF says Palestinian soccer players it targeted served Hamas and Jihad terror groups
description: The IDF claims Palestinian soccer players killed during the war allegedly served as terrorists for Hamas and Islamic Jihad in operational roles.
fullText:
EXCLUSIVE: The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) has told Fox News Digital that soccer players representing the Palestinian Authority it targeted with military strikes were allegedly also working as terrorists for Hamas and Jihadist groups.

"In recent weeks, false claims have been circulated alleging that the IDF deliberately targeted innocent Palestinian soccer players throughout the war," the IDF said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"In light of these claims, the IDF is now revealing the identities of Palestinian soccer players, referees, and coaches who were presented as athletes and innocent civilians, but who in fact served as terrorists in the military wings of the Hamas and Islamic Jihad terrorist organizations in the Gaza Strip, and were struck and eliminated by the IDF during the war.

Idf Claims To Have Taken Out Hamas Commander Who Participated In Oct 7

"Following an intelligence assessment, the IDF can now disclose these terrorists' organizational affiliations and operational roles. In many cases, they were portrayed internationally only as players, with no mention of their affiliation with terrorist organizations."

The IDF identified the players as Mohammed Mohammed Hassan Barakat, Mahmoud Kamal Mohammad Ali Al-Rifi, Mohammed Mansour, Ahmed Mohammed Hassan Abu Al-Atta, Abdullah Riyadh Abdullah Khattab, Ali Mahmoud Ahmed Al-Kurd, Mohammad Emad Ata Hussouna, Tariq Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mohammed Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mahmoud Osama Al-Jazzar and Mohammad Nidal Mohammed Al-Hawajri.

According to the IDF, three of the players were allegedly involved in the Oct. 7, 2023 massacre, and others allegedly served in other terror operations and leadership roles.

"The false campaign portraying the IDF as deliberately targeting members of the soccer community, which was created as part of a broader effort promoted by various actors against the State of Israel, accompanies other false narratives advanced throughout the war," the IDF added.

"In those campaigns, Palestinian terrorists struck and eliminated by the IDF were presented as journalists, humanitarian aid workers, and other civilian professionals, while their involvement in terrorist activity was deliberately concealed in order to promote false claims that they had been targeted because of their civilian professions."

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Permanent Observer Mission of the State of Palestine to the United Nations for a response to the IDF's claims.

Iran Allegedly Used Soccer Clubs To Spy On Citizens, Opposition Group Urges Fifa Ban Regime Before World Cup

The official Palestinian Football Association (PFA) reported earlier this month that about 1,009 members of its athletic community had been killed by IDF forces since the war began in October 2023, according to Al Jazeera.

The IDF's exclusive disclosures come against the backdrop of a fierce diplomatic and public relations battle on the global sports stage.

Click Here For More Sports Coverage On Foxnews.com

For over two years, the Palestinian Football Association, headed by Jibril Rajoub, has aggressively lobbied the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and FIFA to suspend Israel from international competition. Rajoub and the PFA have repeatedly cited the mounting death toll of Palestinian athletes and the destruction of sports infrastructure in Gaza as grounds for an immediate, indefinite ban.

The campaign reached a boiling point at recent FIFA Congresses. However, following extensive legal reviews, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee ultimately rejected the PFA's demands to suspend Israel entirely.

Instead, FIFA recently opted to impose a fine on the Israel Football Association (IFA) for unrelated disciplinary breaches and ordered the Israeli team to display a "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banner during upcoming matches.

However, FIFA did not let the IFA walk away completely untouched. In a compromise measure, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee opted to penalize the Israeli association for unrelated disciplinary breaches.

The IFA was fined 150,000 Swiss francs (approximately $190,700) for multiple breaches of anti-discrimination obligations. Additionally, FIFA ordered the IFA to implement a mandatory anti-discrimination prevention plan and required the Israeli team to prominently display "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banners during upcoming matches.

title: ‘A politically impossible situation’: New poll shows Trump voters losing patience with Iran war
description: The share of MAGA voters who think the war is worth the economic cost has fallen to roughly over a third, down from 50 percent in May, the POLITICO Poll found.
fullText:
Half of President Donald Trump's MAGA base thought the Iran war was worth its economic costs in May. Now, just a little more than a third do.

That precipitous, 13-point drop in just two months, according to a new POLITICO Poll, reveals how the president's most die-hard supporters are starting to agree with the majority of Americans who have long opposed the U.S.' involvement in the war.

Additionally, 37 percent of self-identified MAGA Trump voters say the U.S. should continue its involvement only if it doesn't increase costs, up from 29 percent who said the same in May, according to the survey, conducted by independent polling firm Public First. Nearly 1 in 5 MAGA voters believe that the U.S. should end its war in Iran, regardless of the costs.

That flagging support among the president's most loyal supporters is the clearest sign that Americans' patience is wearing thin as the economy continues to dominate as voters' top concern heading into a critical midterm cycle.

But Trump on Monday gave little indication that he was looking for a quick end to the war. The U.S. military has pounded Iran for the last two weeks, and speaking to reporters on Tuesday the president claimed that Iranian officials "want to desperately meet" but that "until they are ready to meet in a meaningful way we have no interest in even talking."

The White House is "at a maximum level of frustration right now, and I was told by very senior people that the president is now fully aware that the only way to the victory that he wants is completely politically impossible. The American people just will not support the kind of escalation it would take at this point," said one person close to the White House, granted anonymity to discuss private conversations. "We've got more dead Americans and a politically impossible situation."

This souring of the president's base comes amid renewed hostilities between the U.S. and Iran, which have been conducting tit-for-tat strikes after the president earlier this month declared a tentative peace agreement between the two countries "over." Two U.S. servicemembers were killed in Jordan this weekend and gas prices have climbed above $4 per gallon, underscoring the war's impact at home.

The mood among White House staff and their outside allies is grim as the president grapples with finding a path out of an unpopular and expensive war three months from a midterm election expected to turn on cost of living issues.

The president was expected to spend the year on a victory tour on affordability issues, touting his popular tax cuts, Trump accounts for newborns and lower drug prices. Instead, a domestic travel schedule has been pocked by visits to Dover Air Force Base to oversee the dignified transfer of the remains of more than a dozen dead soldiers, and he has during his campaign-style rallies been forced to time and again argue to the American people that the price of war — most salient to higher prices at the pump — was worth ending Iran's nuclear program.

White House aides contend that price bumps are temporary and will quickly return to pre-war levels either when the war ends or when the U.S. has degraded Iran's military capabilities to the point that it can no longer stem the flow of oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.

"What matters most to the American people is having a Commander in Chief who takes bold action to keep them safe, which is exactly what President Trump is doing," said White House spokesperson Olivia Wales. "The President does not make national security decisions based on fluid opinion polls, but on the best interests of the American people."

Still, the latest polling shows that non-MAGA Trump voters are even more down on the war, with only 21 percent believing it is worth higher costs, while 28 percent say that the U.S. shouldn't continue the war regardless of costs.

And the results show the president's voters are broadly comfortable blaming the war for higher prices. When asked why gas prices have increased since the president took office, the majority of respondents blamed the conflict, including 57 percent of MAGA voters and 63 percent of non-MAGA Trump voters.

No other cause — from seasonal demand to environmental regulations limiting drilling — crested 25 percent for the core MAGA group. Those who voted for former Vice President Kamala Harris, on the other hand, also blamed the president's poor handling of the economy and his tariffs, at 55 percent and 52 percent, respectively, for the rise in costs.

Trump's 2024 supporters also are split on whether gas prices would have gone up more, less or the same under a Democratic president compared with now — another indication that Americans are more willing to assign blame to Trump for the state of the economy. Equal shares of Trump voters say that prices would've gone up more under a Democrat (34 percent) and that prices would have gone up the same (35 percent). A notable 20 percent believe prices have gone up more under Trump.

Prominent Republicans, including strategists in key races, have spent the last several months warning about the impact of backlash to the Iran war on their chances of holding the House and the Senate, with frustrations around the war playing out in competitive races across the country.

"This shows that as unpopular as the first round of war was from February to early summer, this second round is even more ridiculous," said Curt Mills, executive director of the American Conservative magazine. "The American people quite simply do not get it, do not understand why we are in this war, and, yes, President Trump is king of the Republican Party. Yes, the president can do a lot and has a lot of latitude with his own supporters, but kings are not invulnerable from political reality."

But Trump on Tuesday said he wasn't concerned.

"They won't have any impact on me. I'm just here to do the right thing. I don't view it as, gee, we're having an election in a period of time. You know, everyone says, oh, I'm doing something before — no, the election — I can't think about that having to do with this," Trump said.

title: Has Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran?
description: He decried endless wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Now he may be stuck in one of his own making
fullText:
He decried endless wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. Now he may be stuck in one of his own making

As Donald Trump launched a war of aggression against Iran nearly five months ago, he insisted Washington would achieve a decisive victory within weeks. Trump and his advisers scoffed at any suggestion that they were breaking repeated campaign promises not to start new conflicts, and to end America’s legacy of forever wars, especially in Afghanistan and Iraq.

In early March, a week into the war, Trump declared he would only agree to “unconditional surrender” by Iran’s theocratic government. He vowed to destroy much of Tehran’s military, called for a popular uprising and touted the overthrow of the regime that rose to power after Iran’s 1979 Islamic revolution. Of course, Trump’s grandiose promises turned out to be the latest chapter in a long history of American imperial hubris.

Not to mention that Trump contradicted multiple pledges he had made to a weary US public that he wouldn’t start new wars during his second presidency. In November 2024, in his election night victory speech from Florida, Trump confidently told Americans: “I’m not going to start a war. I’m going to stop wars.”

Now, the ceasefire he negotiated with Tehran has collapsed and Trump risks dragging the US into yet another forever war – in Iran.

In mid-June, the US and Iran reached a memorandum of understanding that included reopening the strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of the world’s oil and liquid gas supplies transited before Iran shut it during the war. That agreement was effectively a 60-day extension of a ceasefire that was reached in early April, but did not lead to reopening the crucial waterway because Trump imposed a naval blockade against Iranian ships in the region. Trump said the memo “achieves everything we set out to accomplish”, even though it had deferred the most difficult questions – including limitations on Iran’s nuclear program and Tehran’s potential control over of the strait of Hormuz – to future negotiations.

“Ships of the World, start your engines,” Trump wrote on social media when he announced the interim deal last month. “Let the oil flow!”

But each side had divergent interpretations of the agreement, and the deal collapsed after less than a month. Over the past 10 days, tit-for-tat attacks have lurched the region back towards an all-out war. Iran fired on oil tankers that passed through the strait near the coastline of Oman in the waterway’s southern corridor, which the US military has encouraged vessels to use to avoid Iran’s control. The Pentagon has launched daily waves of airstrikes against what it described as Iranian command centers, missile and drone launch sites, air defense systems and communication networks. In recent days, the US also bombed bridges across southern Iran – and Trump has threatened to destroy Iran’s power plants and other civilian infrastructure, which would probably constitute a war crime.

Tehran has retaliated with missile and drone strikes against US military bases across the Middle East, and it threatened to renew attacks on the energy infrastructure of its Gulf neighbors. Trump reimposed a blockade against Iranian shipping, and traffic through the strait of Hormuz has slowed to a trickle. The price of oil reached its highest level in a month, passing $90 a barrel, and once again disrupting the global economy. On Friday, Iranian ballistic missiles struck an airbase in Jordan, killing two US military personnel, with a third missing in action. The Pentagon said another service member had been killed on Saturday in northern Iraq, where the US has bases. Since the war began, 17 US service members have been killed and about 430 wounded.

Trump had previously declared that the killing of US personnel would elicit large-scale American retaliation against Iran. It’s yet another way that Trump has cornered himself into an endless cycle of bombing and military escalation. He has no strategy to turn US military superiority into a lasting political agreement with Tehran, and he has wavered for months between making a grand deal and threatening to restart a full-scale war. For much of the conflict, the scope of negotiations – and overall US policy toward Iran – seemed to depend on Trump’s shifting mood.

For its part, the Iranian regime survived the assassination of its top leaders and withstood weeks of intense bombing by the US and Israel, two of the world’s most powerful militaries. On 28 February, in the conflict’s early hours, joint US-Israeli airstrikes killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and more than 30 other top Iranian officials. The regime’s surviving leadership quickly installed Khamenei’s son, Mojtaba, as the new supreme leader. He is supported by hardline generals of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the country’s most powerful military and economic force.

Iran’s leaders emerged more emboldened and with greater leverage than they had before Trump’s ill-fated war. They realized Tehran could hold the world’s economy hostage by deploying a weapon that is far more effective than its missiles and drones: control over the strait of Hormuz, which allows Iran to disrupt shipments of not just oil and liquified gas, but also key agricultural products such as fertilizer.

And Iran has more economic leverage it could deploy if Trump decides to resume an all-out war. Tehran could pressure the Houthi militia, its ally which controls large parts of Yemen, to disrupt international shipping through the Red Sea, which would further destabilize global energy supplies. After Israel’s invasion of Gaza in 2023, the Houthis began firing missiles and drones at vessels sailing around the Bab el-Mandab strait, where the Red Sea comes closest to Houthi-controlled areas of Yemen. Shipping companies rerouted hundreds of vessels around South Africa, increasing insurance premiums and adding thousands of miles to a freighter’s journey between Asia and Europe.

For now, the Houthis have not fully entered the conflict, but on Monday the militia announced a blockade of Saudi Arabia’s ports and ships. This could put more pressure on energy prices because Saudi Arabia has been exporting millions of barrels of its oil through the Red Sea route, so that it could circumvent Iran’s shuttering of the strait of Hormuz. Tehran can disrupt energy markets in other ways: it could widen its attacks on oil and natural gas infrastructure in its Gulf neighbors, including Kuwait, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.

All of these scenarios would probably cause energy prices to soar, and could trigger a global recession. And yet Trump shows little sign of pulling back from the brink of a wider war with Iran, which could involve the deployment of US ground troops and spread into neighboring countries.

Trump is falling into a potential forever war of his own making.

Mohamad Bazzi is a Guardian US columnist. He is also director of the Center for Near Eastern Studies and a journalism professor at New York University

title: Splatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey
description:
Nintendo Switch 2
The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Splatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.
The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others.
fullText:
Nintendo Switch 2
The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill

Splatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.

The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others. Players start each session on a hideout ship where they can upgrade their equipment and tweak their play style before heading out on bite-sized repeatable missions, and this structure is tailor-made for teaming up with friends or helping out strangers (the game lets you do either), as each person works towards creating their ultimate squid-kid avatar of chaos.

Playing Splatoon Raiders solo feels like a lesser experience for this reason. Its levels are straightforwardly constructed to keep groups together. There is little of the puzzly rush of finding hidden depths with creative use of your tools. Instead, the fun is in putting together your kit. Splatoon’s weapons have always been goofy and playfully designed, but even better is learning how this new entry complements them with a suite of augmentable gadgets. As your options accrete and the game escalates in challenge, Raiders comes to life.

Some may not get that far. Splatoon Raiders takes a long time to make it clear how completely the experience can change with the right kit, and while it offers a decent variety of challenges to tackle, players are meant to replay them. The minimal story, in which you are an unnamed mechanic stranded on a mysterious archipelago with the band Deep Cut (returning from Splatoon 3), is not terribly compelling. Which is surprising for a series with a history of casually revealing outrageous new details about its post-climate apocalypse world and its human-cephalopod inhabitants.

Splatoon is Nintendo at its most offbeat, venturing forth in a direction few other game studios travel. And, according to sales, audiences outside of the company’s native Japan seem to struggle with how to receive it. In this context, Splatoon Raiders feels like a concession: Nintendo taking aside one of its rangier, peculiar creations and asking it to be a bit more normal. This is the biggest surprise about Splatoon Raiders: it is pedestrian. If you’ve played one of dozens of modern cooperative action games, you’ve played this. You have upgraded your gear and compared weapon stats and divined synergies between abilities in order to throw your character into the meat grinder over and over again, watching overwhelming hordes eventually become trivial as your stats improve, your arsenal more varied. There is only so much that Splatoon’s ink-fuelled weaponry and charming DIY design sensibilities can do to elevate this experience. Like its archipelago setting, Splatoon Raiders is far removed from what makes the main series so charming, and what’s left here feels a bit too much like watching paint dry.

Splatoon Raiders is out 23 July; £41.99

title: Romania says ship heading to Ukraine struck off its coast
description: July 21 (Reuters) - A Liberian-flagged vessel sailing from Egypt to Ukraine has been hit off Romania's coast in an attack likely linked to Russia's war on Ukraine, Romanian President Nicusor Dan said
fullText:
July 21 (Reuters) - A Liberian-flagged vessel sailing from Egypt to Ukraine has been hit ‌off Romania's coast in an attack likely ‌linked to Russia's war on Ukraine, Romanian President Nicusor Dan ​said on Tuesday.

This year, Russian and Ukrainian drones have repeatedly breached the airspace and territorial waters of Romania, officials in the European Union and ‌NATO member state ⁠neighbouring Ukraine have said.

"Last night, the LPG Gas Lisbon, a Liberian-flagged vessel, was ⁠hit outside Romanian territorial waters, approximately 20 nautical miles (40 km) off the Romanian coast," Dan wrote ​on X.

Dan ​said Romanian rescuers had ​evacuated the crew, including ‌three injured members, and a tugboat had been dispatched to ensure the vessel did not drift and pose a danger to navigation.

"State institutions are on alert and will clarify the circumstances, ‌causes and responsibilities related to ​this serious incident, most likely ​part of the ​illegal war of aggression of the ‌Russian Federation against Ukraine," Dan ​said.

Russia, which ​launched a full scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, has either denied responsibility for reported ​attacks on ‌Romania, said they were carried out by ​Ukraine, or blamed Ukrainian "provocation".

title: Ukraine's weapons makers fear a costly slowdown after their tech champion was pushed out of the defense ministry
description: Defense industry sources worry about what Mykhailo Fedorov's dismissal could mean for battlefield innovation at a pivotal moment in the war.
fullText:
Volodymyr Zelenskyy dismissed Mykhailo Fedorov, Ukraine's reformist defense minister, last week.

The move has caused unease among Ukrainian manufacturers, who praised Fedorov's innovative approach.

Industry sources said they are concerned about the potential impact on Ukraine's war efforts.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy's surprise dismissal of his reformist defense minister has sent shockwaves through the country, causing unease and anxiety as Kyiv enjoyed a wave of fresh momentum on the battlefield.

Ukrainian defense industry sources described the removal of 35-year-old Mykhailo Fedorov last week, after just six months on the job, as a major disappointment for the country's powerhouse military-technology sector, expressing concerns about serious problems down the road.

A Ukrainian defense tech company representative who asked only to be identified as Anton said that Fedorov "was driving innovations, visionary goals, and very brave practical operations that gave President Zelenskyy his cards."

Anton said that Fedorov was proactive rather than reactive in his efforts to substantially overhaul Ukrainian weapons development, tactics, and doctrine, adding that "he was fulfilling today's needs and gaps while building tomorrow's relevance and superiority to ensure our victory."

Fedorov, who previously served as Ukraine's minister for digital transformation and is known for his Silicon Valley-style approach to the war, was appointed by Zelenskyy in January to oversee the defense ministry.

He quickly pushed to dramatically reform Ukraine's military, including placing greater emphasis on deploying drones and reshaping the contracting system. Fedorov also sought to overhaul the defense industry to increase development and make the procurement process more transparent and efficient.

Russia's invasion has turned Ukraine into a central hub for robotics and drone innovation. Hundreds of companies are building new technologies — from drones to electronic warfare devices — for the military, and many have their eyes set on future exports.

During Fedorov's tenure, Ukrainian firms enjoyed a much more transparent procurement process with the government, Anton said. He described collaborating with the defense ministry as a "professional and very productive" dialogue, "something we never could imagine before."

He said that Fedorov transformed the defense industry to meet the challenges of a changing battlefield, one that is becoming increasingly autonomous.

Since January, Ukrainian forces have been significantly ramping up their long-range drone strikes on Russian oil facilities and have used mid-range drones to hammer Russian logistics in the rear. The two campaigns have given Kyiv a sense of optimism and stymied Moscow's battlefield momentum while fueling its own.

Defense manufacturers see Ukraine's victories over the past few months as a testament to the drone-forward approach of Fedorov and his team.

The CEO of robotic systems maker Ark Robotics said that "policies supporting rapid deployment, streamlined procurement, and continuous battlefield feedback created the conditions for rapid technological progress."

Fedorov's emphasis on rapid technological change and decentralized innovation sometimes clashed with the military's more conventional command structure under Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi. Zelenskyy said the rift between the two had become a problem.

Zelenskyy omitted Fedorov from his government in a major reshuffle last week. The former defense minister said he was pushed out, and while he declined a government advisory role, it's unclear whether he could stay on in another capacity.

The dismissal caused confusion and anger among Ukrainian civilians and soldiers, and has stoked anxiety within the defense industry over the future of procurement and manufacturing priorities.

As defense minister, "Fedorov built a system where only equipment that actually worked and delivered results made it to the front," a Ukrainian manufacturer said, adding that "he created a competitive environment: manufacturers competed on quality, lowered their prices, and still met the front's real needs."

They said they worry about a potential backslide to "manual, top-down control."

Another Ukrainian manufacturer said they are "deeply concerned" as a producer, not only because of Fedorov's dismissal but also because Kyiv has gone through three defense ministers in a little over a year.

"This creates an unpredictable frame for work," they said. "Changing a minister and his team usually takes time to adjust all processes. This means that the adoption of necessary decisions can be slower for some time. Thus, this can affect our work as well."

Zelenskyy said that he asked Maj. Gen. Yevhen Khmara, the acting head of Ukraine's SBU internal security agency, to lead the defense ministry in the interim.

Khmara previously led the SBU's elite Alpha unit, which has been the tip of the spear for Ukraine's deep strikes into Russia, so drone innovation may remain a priority for the defense ministry.

The Ark Robotics CEO said it remains to be seen whether Ukraine has the "institutional, strategic commitment to realize the same vision going forward and accelerate the changes" that Fedorov started.

Proper policies will enable defense innovation to move at the rapid pace required in a modern war, they said. "Our take is that being radical in implementing widespread use of unmanned systems is an existential question for Ukraine."

Without Fedorov at the helm, Ukrainian defense manufacturers don't see their efforts completely collapsing. However, they are worried about a potential change in pace that could prove harmful at a pivotal moment in the war.

"What happens to the industry, army, and country without Minister Fedorov?" Anton said. "Heaven will not fall on earth; the industry will work, as we all have a strong motivation to win this war, or at least not to lose."

"We will continue doing our best, as well as the guys in the trenches," he said, but there's a risk that "we will lose the pace of development."

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: Houthis announce Saudi naval blockade, threatening new front in US-Iran war
description: DUBAI/RIYADH, July 20 (Reuters) - Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis said on Monday they would impose a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, opening a potential new front against the United States in its war wit...
fullText:
DUBAI/RIYADH, July 20 (Reuters) - Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis said on Monday they would impose a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, opening a potential new front against the United States in its war with Iran and raising the threat to global energy supplies and trade beyond the Gulf.

The escalation comes after one of the deadliest periods in the war for U.S. troops. The ‌Pentagon on Monday identified two U.S. soldiers who were killed when Iran attacked an American base in Jordan on Friday, while they also said they had found unidentified remains that could be those of a third soldier ‌reported as missing in action. Separately, a fourth service member was killed in northern Iraq during the "controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone."

After the Houthis announced their blockade, the Saudi-led coalition in Yemen said in a statement that it would respond to them with force, and ​also that it began implementing protection measures for its ships moving through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, a key export route for Saudi oil after the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

The Houthis made their announcement despite signs that Tehran and Washington want to resume diplomacy to halt a worsening cycle of attacks that has all but wrecked a fragile interim agreement signed last month. Iran's Foreign Ministry said mediators had presented "proposals" to Tehran, signalling that diplomatic contacts remain active, but gave no details.

Oil prices rose briefly after the Houthi statement but later fell back as traders held out hope of a diplomatic breakthrough. The insurance cost of shipping goods through the Red Sea rose on the increased risks for merchant shipping.

Iran had been pressing the Houthis to close the Bab el-Mandeb ‌gateway to the Red Sea if the U.S. continued to attack Iranian power infrastructure.

A ⁠full closure of the strait would reduce global oil supply by 7% as it would leave most of Saudi oil exports unable to leave the region. The disruption would add to the huge cut to oil flows from the war in the Gulf, which has already reduced shipments by 10% of global supply.

In their statement, the Houthis' armed forces said they were declaring "a maritime embargo against the criminal ⁠Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye', effective immediately" in response to what they called "an unjust and oppressive siege" imposed on Yemen by the Saudis.

DIPLOMATIC PUSH TO RESTORE CEASEFIRE

A senior Iranian official told Reuters on Monday that Tehran had received a proposal from mediators for a 10-day ceasefire in efforts to salvage the interim deal, intended to pave the way to a lasting agreement to end the war that began on February 28 with U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran.

Neither the ministry nor the official who spoke to Reuters gave ​any ​details on the putative ceasefire discussions.

Two Pakistani government sources said separately that Iranian Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni had asked Pakistan to resume its mediation ​role in the conflict. He later arrived in Islamabad for further talks.

The diplomatic push followed ‌another night of U.S. strikes on Iranian cities and attacks by Iran's Revolutionary Guards on U.S. military assets across the region. U.S. Central Command said Monday afternoon U.S. time that it had begun another round of strikes on Iran.

President Donald Trump, under mounting political pressure at home over rising gasoline prices since the war began and Iran effectively closed the vital Strait of Hormuz, defended the latest strikes on Iran as honoring the deaths of American service members killed in recent Iranian attacks.

"Every time Iran kills an American Soldier they will pay for that killing many times over! This directive has been passed on to Secretary of War, Pete Hegseth, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, Daniel Caine, and every Leader in the Military," Trump wrote on Truth Social on Monday.

The war has killed thousands of people, mostly in Iran and Lebanon, part of which has been occupied by Israeli forces following attacks on Israel by Hezbollah fighters who said they were acting in solidarity with Tehran.

Lebanese ‌President Joseph Aoun is due to meet Trump at the White House on Tuesday to present a plan on how to disarm Iran-aligned ​Hezbollah and secure an Israeli withdrawal.

Israel also still has forces in Gaza following two years of war ignited by the Palestinian militant group Hamas' attack ​on southern Israeli communities on October 7, 2023. Hamas said on Monday it had named top negotiator Khalil Al-Hayya ​as its overall leader, a move that an analyst said could signal a tougher stance by the group towards calls for it to disarm.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said ‌two oil tankers had exploded after attempting to transit the strait via an "unsafe" route. On Sunday, ​the IRGC had said two ships were involved in an "accident" ​in the same area. It was unclear whether the two incidents were related.

Separately, a vessel was struck by an unknown projectile off Oman's coast, overlooking the Strait of Hormuz, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said.

In Iran, explosions were reported in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini. One person was killed and several wounded ​southwest of Tabriz, according to state news agency IRNA.

The IRGC said it targeted U.S. aircraft ‌at Jordan's Aqaba airport with ballistic missiles, as well as military assets at Kuwait's Al-Adiri camp and Ali Al Salem Air Base, and positions in Syria.

Sirens sounded in Bahrain throughout Monday. Kuwait's army said early ​Tuesday that air defenses were again intercepting Iranian drones.

title: Iran war pushes gas prices back above $4 per gallon
description: With the war in Iran continuing to escalate, the national average gas price for a gallon of regular gas is now $4, according to AAA. Experts tell NBC News consumers can expect higher prices in the day...
fullText:

title: Michigan candidate Abdul El-Sayed dodges when pressed on controversial remarks about death of Khamenei
description: Abdul El-Sayed dodged Jake Tapper's questions about remarks on Dearborn residents being saddened by Ali Khamenei's death during the U.S.-Iran conflict.
fullText:
Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed sidestepped questions from CNN's Jake Tapper on Sunday after the host confronted him over past remarks suggesting "a lot of people" in Dearborn, Michigan, were saddened by the death of Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei.

Tapper pointed to audio first highlighted by The Washington Free Beacon from an event held shortly after Khamenei's death during the U.S.-Iran conflict. In the recording, El-Sayed said there were "a lot of people in Dearborn who are sad."

Michigan Senate Race: El-sayed's Ties To Extremist Fuel Antisemitism Concerns Among Jewish Voters

"I thought that quote was interesting, because the ayatollah has killed tens of thousands of innocent Iranians, not to mention the proxies that Iran has funded that have killed innocent civilians all over the Middle East," Tapper said on CNN's "State of the Union."

"Why were you worried about offending people who support the ayatollah?"

El-Sayed, a Muslim progressive vying to succeed retiring Sen. Gary Peters, D-Mich., declined to directly answer the question, instead criticizing Tapper for revisiting his past remarks rather than focusing on the U.S.-Iran conflict and its impact on Michigan.

Trump's 60-Day Iran Deal Reaches Halfway Mark As Ceasefire Collapses Into Escalating War

"Jake, on a day where we're mourning the loss of two more service members in a war we never should have fought, I think all of us ought to step back and ask whether or not everybody in Michigan, frankly, everybody in America, shouldn't be sad about the fact that this president, backed by the Prime Minister of a foreign government, took us to a war we had no business fighting in," he said.

He went on to argue that Tapper should have instead been asking about the recent deaths of U.S. service members in the Middle East — which Tapper had mentioned at the start of his question — and whether the Iran conflict should have occurred and the economic impact of the war on Michiganders.

Michigan Senate Candidate Abdul El-sayed Dodges Question On Whether He Still Supports Defunding The Police

"I'm running for Senate in Michigan, and I think, here in Michigan, all of us are probably sad about the fact that we went to war that we never should have fought," El-Sayed said.

"I'd rather our money be spent here," he said.

Tapper replied, "I was specifically asking you about comments you didn't want to make because you didn't want to offend people in Dearborn, which is in Michigan."

title: 19-year-old soldier from Texas killed in base attack in Jordan
description: Gonzales was supporting Operation Inherent Resolve, which assists partner forces secure a lasting defeat of ISIS, according to the Dept. of War.
fullText:
A Texas soldier was one of two service members killed in a weekend attack on a base in Jordan.

Pvt. Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas, and 1st Lt. Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, were killed in action after a July 17 attack at Muwaffaq Salti Air Base, Jordan, the Department of War announced in a news release.

The two were active-duty Soldiers who were supporting Operation Inherent Resolve, which assists partner forces in securing a lasting defeat of ISIS.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth reacted to the attack on X and wrote, "Godspeed, heroes. Their sacrifice only stiffens our resolve."

The incident is still under investigation, the Department of War said in the news release.

Pvt. Gonzales was assigned to 1st Battalion, 57th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 52nd Air Defense Artillery Brigade, 10th Army Air Missile Defense Command, Ansbach, Germany.

1st Lt. Feehan was assigned to 2nd Battalion, 55th Air Defense Artillery Regiment, 32nd Army Air Missile Defense Command, Fort Bragg, North Carolina.

Natassia Paloma may be reached at npaloma@gannett.com, @NatassiaPaloma on X; natassia_paloma on Instagram, and Natassia Paloma on Facebook.

title: Ukraine unleashes more than 400 drones near Moscow in one of war's biggest attacks
description: More than 400 drones targeted Moscow in one of Ukraine's largest attacks, striking an oil depot and logistics facilities more than 250 miles from the border.
fullText:
Ukraine launched one of its largest drone attacks of the war against Moscow and the surrounding region overnight, striking an oil depot and logistics facilities more than 250 miles from the Ukrainian border, according to Ukrainian and Russian officials.

Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said more than 400 Ukrainian drones were launched toward the Moscow region between Sunday evening and early Monday, including 85 that were destroyed while approaching the Russian capital. Russia's Defense Ministry separately claimed that its forces intercepted 381 drones across Russian regions, the Black Sea and the Sea of Azov.

The Russian claims could not be independently verified by Fox News Digital.

At least 10 people were wounded in the Moscow region, including three Chinese nationals, according to regional authorities cited by Reuters. Seven of the injuries were reported in Domodedovo, Russia, where officials said a residential building was struck and a fire broke out at an industrial park near one of Moscow's main airports.

The scale and reach of the operation suggest Ukraine is moving beyond occasional symbolic attacks on Moscow and building a sustained campaign against the infrastructure that supports Russia's war.

Ukraine Launches What Appears To Be One Of Its Largest Drone Attacks Against Russia: Report

Ukrainian attacks on Russian refineries had doubled since the beginning of 2026, Reuters reported, contributing to refinery shutdowns and reduced production of gasoline, diesel and jet fuel.

President Volodymyr Zelenskyy confirmed that Ukrainian forces targeted logistics facilities and an oil depot in the Moscow region.

"Our long-range sanctions have produced results against logistics facilities and an oil depot," Zelenskyy wrote on X, adding that the targets were more than 400 kilometers (roughly 250 miles) from Ukraine's border. "We are responding, justly, to every Russian strike against our cities and communities."

Wildberries, a company known as "Russia's Amazon," said its logistics center in the Podolsk district of Russia was temporarily evacuated as a precaution before resuming normal operations, East2West News reported. Ukraine has accused the company of selling supplies used by the Russian military.

Russia Unleashes Nearly 600 Missiles And Drones On Kyiv In Deadliest Strike Since May

Ksenia Svetlova, associate fellow at Chatham House policy institute based in London, argued that the disruption caused by strikes on fuel infrastructure and companies such as Wildberries is unlikely, by itself, to change President Vladimir Putin's approach to the war.

"The hardship of the Russian citizen who suffers because of fuel shortages, the strike on a Wildberries warehouse, or forced recruitment does not interest him," Svetlova said. "The war machine is operating at full force, and he has neither the desire nor the ability to stop it."

Svetlova said Putin remains reluctant to announce another mass mobilization after large numbers of young Russians fled the country during the Kremlin's previous call-up. At the same time, she said, Russia's military still needs additional troops after recruiting heavily from its prison population and is increasingly pressuring ordinary civilians accused of relatively minor offenses to sign military contracts.

The sweeping Ukrainian operation followed one of Russia's largest ballistic missile attacks of the war against Kyiv. Ukraine's air force said Russia launched 41 missiles of various types in the overnight barrage, which killed at least one person and wounded 16, Reuters reported Sunday.

The Russian attack also spread into the Black Sea, where a Russian missile attack on the Guinea-Bissau-flagged cargo ship Golden Leo killed 10 people near Odesa, Reuters reported Monday.

'A New Kind Of War': Inside Ukraine's Hidden Factories Mass-producing Combat Drones

Ukrainian officials said the vessel, which was carrying corn, was struck by three cruise missiles and caught fire. Nine crew members and a Ukrainian maritime pilot were killed, while eight other crew members were rescued. The ship's crew included Indian and Syrian nationals, according to Reuters.

The escalation comes as Zelenskyy faces growing domestic pressure over his removal of Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, a 35-year-old reformer credited with expanding Ukraine's drone capabilities and seeking to modernize military procurement and management. Fedorov's supporters viewed him as a rare official willing to challenge entrenched practices, improve oversight of defense spending and push a technology-centered approach to the war.

Thousands of demonstrators gathered following Fedorov's dismissal, demanding his reinstatement and the removal of commander-in-chief Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi. Protesters have accused Syrskyi of resisting modernization and retaining a rigid command structure that they say contributes to high troop losses. Syrskyi has defended his wartime record and called for unity.

Fedorov publicly accused Syrskyi of blocking his initiatives and sabotaging his work. Zelenskyy said their relationship had deteriorated so badly that the two could no longer work together, but provided little additional explanation for removing Fedorov.

Zelenskyy indicated Monday that consultations over Ukraine's military leadership were continuing, saying he had spoken with Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi about ensuring that the experience of every branch of Ukraine's defense forces was applied in a coordinated manner. He did not announce any decision on Syrskyi's future.

title: An excavation in Taiwan searches for remains of Japanese soldiers lost in WWII ship sinking
description: Heads bowed, experts offered their respects on Monday ahead of an excavation aimed at recovering the remains of Japanese soldiers from World War II in southern Taiwan.  The site is linked to the sinki...
fullText:
PINGTUNG, Taiwan (AP) — Heads bowed, experts offered their respects on Monday ahead of an excavation aimed at recovering the remains of Japanese soldiers from World War II in southern Taiwan.

The site is linked to the sinking of a military transport vessel and other ships in the Bashi Channel after they were attacked by U.S. forces in 1944. According to historian Kazuko Tachi, who is also an activist involved in the recovery efforts, more than 4,700 soldiers and crew members died while 65 survived.

Taiwan, a self-governing island claimed by China as its territory, was a Japanese colony from 1895 until the end of the war.

"It is believed that many bodies from the sunken vessel reached this shore," said Tatsuaki Inoue, representing the Japan Association for the Recovery and Repatriation of War Casualties.

The excavation in Hengchun Township is expected to continue for a week. If any remains believed to be Japanese are found and DNA samples can be extracted, they will be sent to Japan during a future mission.

Japan enacted a law in 2016 to accelerate the recovery of war remains, an effort that has been extended through 2030 and includes expanded DNA testing.

Official Japanese records say about 41,900 soldiers who fought for Japan during WWII died in Taiwan. Around 26,300 bodies have been recovered and 15,600 remain missing.

The last excavation in Taiwan took place in 1975, when 242 bodies were found.

"We feel very sorry that we kept them waiting for such a long time," Inoue said. "But on the other hand, we are very happy that we came to be able to excavate in search of their remains."

Historians, residents and Japanese volunteers living in Taiwan spent years conducting interviews and collecting historical records to establish what might have happened to those washed ashore.

They believe the bodies may have been gathered by residents and placed together in a rocky coastal area near the discharge outlet of Taiwan's Third Nuclear Power Plant, where they may still lie.

More than 2.4 million Japanese soldiers died overseas during WWII. The remains of approximately 1 million have yet to be recovered.

In response to public demand, the Japanese government implemented three plans to recover remains between the 1950s and the 1970s, said Akira Nishimura, professor of religious studies at the University of Tokyo.

"For bereaved families who have not received the remains of their loved ones, the lack of certainty regarding where and how their relatives met their end — and the feeling that they were unable to give them a proper sendoff — is likely one of the main reasons why the recovery of remains continues even 80 years later," Nishimura said.

He added that religious organizations and volunteers have played a key role in recovering remains and holding memorial services, offering comfort to bereaved families while helping preserve the memory of those who never returned.

In Pingtung, where Monday's excavation began, two sites honor Japan's war dead.

A monument dedicated to the spirits of Japanese service members who perished stands beside Chaoyin Temple, where an inscription reads: "We pray for the peace of all humanity in the world."

The temple is dedicated to those who died in the Bashi Channel during the war. Inside stands a Japanese flag beside a statue of the Buddhist Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha, who is believed to guide and protect the souls of the departed.

Japan's wartime losses were unusual in that many soldiers and sailors died at sea, leaving their remains unrecovered, said Beatrice Trefalt, associate professor of Japanese studies at Monash University in Australia.

"Because of the enormous amount of uncertainty about who died or not, or how, the search for remains has been a constant in postwar Japanese history," Trefalt said.

She added that many families believe the government has a responsibility to acknowledge those who were conscripted and died during the conflict.

Tachi learned about the fate of the Japanese soldiers who died in the Bashi Channel only after moving to Taiwan. After locals recounted that history, she decided to get involved in recovering their remains.

"Those Taiwanese uncles taught me the importance of holding a memorial service for the war dead," Tachi said. "It's been more than 80 years since the war ended but I believe it is important to pray for the war dead, which I will keep on doing."

Hernández reported from Beijing. AP journalists Mayuko Ono and Mari Yamaguchi in Tokyo contributed.

title: 'We want land invasion': Iran mouthpiece touts new missile, boasts decades of war prep
description: Regime mouthpiece Seyed Mohammad Marandi claims Iran welcomes a U.S. ground invasion and has introduced a new missile as strikes hit Khorramabad.
fullText:
Tehran has spent decades preparing for — and welcoming — a U.S. ground invasion, a senior Iranian official has claimed, as fresh explosions rocked the region on the eighth day of renewed U.S.-Iran strikes.

Seyed Mohammad Marandi, an Iranian American academic identified as a regime "mouthpiece," also boasted that Tehran had just launched a "new missile" while decentralizing and burying its arsenal deep underground to withstand sustained American air campaigns.

"The Americans are planning an invasion. I'm not saying it will necessarily happen. I'm not saying it will necessarily happen in the next few days. But they have plans for an invasion," Marandi, who served as an advisor to Iran's nuclear negotiation team during recent talks with the U.S. in Pakistan, said in an interview with political analyst Glenn Diesen.

I've Studied Military Strategy. Trump Now Faces Iran's Oldest Battlefield Trick

"Whether they carry them out or not, we'll see. But the Iranians, as you pointed out, the focus of Iranian strikes is on Bahrain and Kuwait, and that's because that's where most American assets have been placed for a ground invasion," Diesen said.

Marandi's comments came as the conflict expanded across western and southern Iran in the run-up to Saturday.

Iran also launched missile and drone salvos targeting U.S.-linked facilities in Jordan, Kuwait and Bahrain.

Jordanian officials confirmed they intercepted 10 missiles early Saturday, while authorities in Kuwait reported casualties and severe damage to a domestic power and desalination plant.

In response, U.S. airstrikes struck bridges, a key road tunnel, surveillance outposts and reinforced underground weapons storage facilities.

Multiple explosions also rocked the city of Khorramabad in Iran's Lorestan province. Iranian state media claimed the blasts were caused by U.S. missiles launched from regional bases in Kuwait, according to reports.

Trump Says Iran's Missiles 'Aren't The Problem' After White House Made Them Central To War Rationale

Khorramabad houses the Imam Ali Missile Base, one of Tehran's primary underground silo facilities engineered to store and deploy liquid-fueled Shahab-3 medium-range ballistic missiles, according to Washington's Nuclear Threat Initiative.

The complex was damaged by Israeli airstrikes during the 2025 campaign targeting Tehran's military and nuclear infrastructure.

Marandi also detailed what he described as a resilient, subterranean network central to Tehran's military strategy.

"When Iran wants to fire missiles or drones, they don't put them inside buildings or in piles somewhere. They take out whatever they need," Marandi said. "They fire them, and then they go back to their bases, or they take out different missiles and drones, spread them out in different areas, put them apart from one another so that the Americans cannot bomb them in large numbers."

He added: "Everything has been spread out in Iran and placed underground."

The latest escalation between the U.S. and Iran ended a recently signed memorandum of understanding (MOU) to establish a 60-day ceasefire and facilitate nuclear talks.

The key issue has centered on control of the Strait of Hormuz, with the strategic waterway turning into the conflict's main battleground.

Trump's 60-Day Iran Deal Reaches Halfway Mark As Ceasefire Collapses Into Escalating War

President Donald Trump ordered renewed airstrikes within weeks of the truce, or MOU, being implemented, and on July 14, he floated the possibility of launching a ground campaign.

According to Marandi, Iran's military command is actively counting on a ground scenario and has since introduced a new weapon to the theater.

"Iran has that advantage, and of course, it has been accumulating missiles and drones for decades now, and it is doing so even now," Marandi claimed.

"They, I think, last night introduced a new missile to the war," he said of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRCG).

"The Iranians are hitting very hard, and they are prepared for a land war. They've been preparing for decades."

Marandi concluded by claiming that Tehran views a U.S. ground operation as the most direct path to ending the conflict on its own terms.

"The Iranians — we discussed this during the 40-day war before on your show — when I said that the Iranians actually want the Americans to carry out a land invasion, and the same is true now, because they felt that if there was a land invasion, they would win," he told Diesen.

Original article source: 'We want land invasion': Iran mouthpiece touts new missile, boasts decades of war prep
